articles.headline: The Elmore Correspondence: the American Anti-Slavery Society's Reply to Hon. F. H. Elmore's Questions on Slavery
articles.author: James G. Birney (corresponding secretary, American Anti-Slavery Society)
articles.summary: The Freeman's front page carries the celebrated correspondence between Hon. Franklin H. Elmore, member of Congress from South Carolina, and the American Anti-Slavery Society, in which James G. Birney (the Society's corresponding secretary) answers Elmore's series of formal questions about the Society's membership, funds, publications and objects. The page reprints Elmore's letters and Birney's point-by-point replies defending the abolitionists' character and the constitutionality of their petitioning, together with resolutions of an anti-slavery meeting (J. Fulton, Jr., Recording Secretary). A predominantly white-abolitionist and Congressional document, recorded for context; named Black Philadelphians do not appear on this page.

articles.headline: Debate in the House of Representatives on the Right of Petition and the Annexation of Texas
articles.summary: Across the front pages of the July 12, 19 and 26 numbers the Freeman reprints extended proceedings of the U.S. House of Representatives on the reference and treatment of anti-slavery petitions and the proposed annexation of Texas, with John Quincy Adams (Mr. Adams of Massachusetts) sparring at length with the chairman and with members from South Carolina and Virginia over the House's handling of petitions and the report of the Committee on Foreign Affairs. Reprinted as national context for the anti-slavery cause; no Black Philadelphia roster on these pages.

articles.headline: The Assembling for Pennsylvania Hall: Decorations, the Flag of Hayti, and the Portraits of Boyer and Petion
articles.summary: The Freeman reprints and rebuts a hostile account of the gathering that dedicated Pennsylvania Hall in May 1838, in which the Committee of the building had blended private invitations with a call in the public papers, inviting participants without distinction of color and from beyond the city and Commonwealth. The account dwells on the mixed-race 'celebration' and on the decorations of the room — 'the national flag of Hayti, and the portraits of Boyer and Petion' — and on the presence of the Haytien commissioners, then being received with honor at Paris (a dinner given for them on the 25th of May by Dutrone, a founder of the French abolition society, with Passy, Odillon Barrot, Las Casas and others). Recorded as part of the Philadelphia Pennsylvania Hall record.

articles.headline: Benjamin Lundy Leaves Philadelphia to Revive the Genius of Universal Emancipation in Illinois
articles.summary: The Freeman reports that on Third-day (Tuesday) morning at 8 o'clock Benjamin Lundy left the city for Pittsburg, on his way to Illinois, to continue the publication of his old periodical, the Genius of Universal Emancipation — whose press and papers had been destroyed in the burning of Pennsylvania Hall — the first number of a new volume having just been issued in Illinois. The editor wishes him a long life of usefulness in the cause of the slave.

events.name: Humane Mechanic Society meeting: committee for the British West India Emancipation celebration (Germantown, Aug 1, 1838)
events.location: Philadelphia (celebration to be held in Germantown)
events.description: At a meeting of the Humane Mechanic Society held July 4th, 1838, it was unanimously resolved that the Society celebrate the anniversary of the British West India Emancipation with a Temperance Festival on August 1, 1838. A committee of nine was appointed to make the arrangements, and it was resolved to hold the celebration in Germantown; tickets one dollar, to be had of the committee. The committee (with their Philadelphia addresses as printed): James Gibbons, 193 Pine Street; James McCrummell, 266 North Third Street; Parris Salters, No. 2 Elizabeth Street; Cyrus B. Miller, 44 N. 4th Street; James Cornish, Pine Street below 10th; James C. White, Old York Road above Noble Street; Nathaniel W. Depee, 334 South Street; Daniel Colly, 189 S. 6th Street; and Wm. C. West, 219 Lombard Street. A note adds that addresses would be delivered by William Whipper, of Columbia, and others. A gold roster of named free Black Philadelphians organizing the First-of-August emancipation celebration.
attendees: committee (Old York Road above Noble St)

events.name: Anti-Slavery Society of the City and County of Philadelphia — Board of Managers stated meeting
events.location: Philadelphia (next meeting at Sandiford Hall, Haines Street)
events.description: At a stated meeting of the Board of Managers of the Anti-Slavery Society of the City and County of Philadelphia, held on the 21st inst. (July 21, 1838), it was resolved that the next stated meeting of the Society — which by the Constitution would take place on the 14th of August — be held at Sandiford Hall, in Haines Street, at 10 o'clock A.M., and that the various auxiliary societies be requested to appoint delegates to attend. Signed J. L. Peirce, Secretary of the Board of Managers.

events.name: Sabbath school teachers and directors meeting to arrange the First-of-August celebration
events.location: Basement of the Second Presbyterian Church, St. Mary's Street, Philadelphia
events.description: At a meeting held July 16th, 1838, by the Sabbath school teachers and directors in the basement story of the Second Presbyterian Church, in St. Mary's Street, to take into consideration the celebration of the First of August (the anniversary of British West India Emancipation), a chairman and secretary were chosen and it was unanimously resolved to celebrate the day, the participants to assemble at their usual place of meeting and proceed thence in a body. Those friendly to the cause of Sabbath schools and to the object of the Vigilant Association were invited to attend. (The presiding officers' names were not legibly recoverable from the scan.)

events.name: Anti-Slavery meeting broken up by a mob, Ben Salem township, Bucks County
events.location: Ben Salem (Bensalem) township, Bucks County, Pennsylvania (in the woods)
events.description: An anti-slavery meeting was held in Bensalem township, Bucks County, in the woods, at which Lewis C. Gunn spoke and Robert Purvis made a few remarks (a second speaker's name was not learned). Between two and three hundred persons were present, but the meeting showed symptoms of misrule: some thirty or forty rioters provided themselves with clubs and assembled on the ground, making loud declarations of how they 'would fight the [Blacks],' so that the reporter could not fully hear what was said. Recorded for Robert Purvis's presence and the pattern of mob disruption of abolitionist gatherings in the summer of 1838.

events.name: Formation of new anti-slavery societies at Upper Dublin and Byberry
events.location: Upper Dublin (Montgomery County) and Byberry township (Philadelphia County), Pennsylvania
events.description: The Freeman reports the formation of new local anti-slavery societies: the Friends' Anti-Slavery Society of Upper Dublin, Montgomery County, where an address was delivered (a Mr. Miller of the Junior Anti-Slavery Society of Philadelphia figures in the account); and a society formed in Byberry township, Philadelphia County, with Samuel Kirk, President, and Jesse Townsend, Secretary. The number of members in these societies was as yet small but growing.

events.name: Pennsylvania Lyceum, Fourth Annual Meeting (order of exercises)
events.description: The Freeman prints the order of exercises for the Fourth Annual meeting of the Pennsylvania Lyceum, to be held at Lancaster, Pa., commencing August 7th, 1838. The multi-day program of essays, lectures and discussions names, among the participants, Mr. S. W. Fuller of Philadelphia, Mr. John Simmons of Philadelphia (on Conchology), Mr. N. Dodge of Mount Joy, Lancaster County (on Female Education), Mr. J. W. Forney of Lancaster (on the State of Pennsylvania), Dr. W. L. Atlee of Lancaster (on Aerolites), Dr. A. Thomas of Lancaster (on Astronomy), Dr. J. L. Peirce and Mr. Philip Cregar (on Physiology in popular education), Dr. Wm. Reynolds, and Messrs. John M. Coleman and L. H. Parsons. A white literary/scientific lyceum; recorded for its named Philadelphia and Lancaster participants.
attendees: lecturer on Female Education (of Mount Joy)
discussion on Moral Philosophy vs Natural Science
